content_preview.title: DOE Deploys $1.9 Billion SPARK Program to Upgrade US Transmission Grid Through Reconductoring Instead of New Construction
content_preview.summary: The US Department of Energy has opened a $1.9 billion funding round under its SPARK program to accelerate grid modernization by replacing aging transmission wires with advanced conductors, a strategy that can double line capacity at half the cost of building new infrastructure.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The US Department of Energy's Office of Electricity on March 12 opened applications for approximately $1.9 billion in grid infrastructure funding under a new program called [SPARK (Speed to Power Through Accelerated Reconductoring and other Key Advanced Transmission Technology Upgrades)](https://www.energy.gov/articles/energy-department-announces-19b-investment-critical-grid-infrastructure-reduce-electricity). The initiative, drawn from the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, re...

editor_notes:
Well-structured News article on a significant federal infrastructure initiative. Summary accurately reflects content. Word count (832) appropriate for News. Good use of 'What We Know / What We Don't Know' structure that avoids speculation while acknowledging uncertainty.

source_verification:
- energy.gov: CONFIRMED. $1.9B SPARK program, GRIP round 3, $10.5B total, Secretary Wright and Assistant Secretary Jereza quotes confirmed, concept papers April 2, full apps May 20, August selections.
- pnas.org: HTTP 403 (academic paywall). Fetched via Archive.org during automated review. PNAS paper on reconductoring capacity doubling. DOI is legitimate.
- utilitydive.com: HTTP 403 on manual fetch. Fetched via Archive.org during automated review. Industry coverage of SPARK program with reconductoring timeline data.
